narrative:
First Warn Forecast
Friday, June 26, 2026
Flash Flood Threat Continues Today
A Few Strong to Severe Storms Possible This Afternoon
Dangerous Heat Builds This Weekend

A First Warn Weather Alert Day remains in effect as another round of heavy rain and thunderstorms moves across the region. While many communities have already received significant rainfall over the past 24 hours, additional rounds of showers and thunderstorms today will increase the risk of flash flooding, especially in areas that have already been soaked. A Flood Watch remains in effect through this evening, and some locations could pick up another one to two inches of rain before the system finally begins to move away.

A stationary front remains draped across central Missouri into southwest Illinois, acting as a focus for repeated thunderstorm development. Deep tropical moisture continues streaming northward into the boundary, creating an atmosphere capable of producing extremely efficient rainfall. Rain chances will be highest this morning, with widespread showers and thunderstorms expected to continue through the early afternoon. Additional redevelopment is likely later today, especially across southeast Missouri and southwest Illinois.

The greatest concern continues to be flash flooding. Rainfall rates may become intense at times, and storms that repeatedly move over the same locations could quickly overwhelm creeks, streams, urban drainage systems, and low-water crossings. Areas that experienced heavy rainfall Thursday will be particularly vulnerable today, as the ground has become saturated and will be unable to absorb much additional rainfall. While most locations may receive another half inch to an inch of rain, localized totals approaching two inches remain possible where thunderstorms repeatedly track over the same communities.

There is also a conditional severe weather threat developing this afternoon between noon and 6:00 p.m. Forecast confidence remains somewhat uncertain because widespread clouds and morning rainfall may limit how much the atmosphere can recover. However, if enough sunshine develops, temperatures will warm and instability will increase, allowing thunderstorms to strengthen during the afternoon. The primary severe weather hazard will be damaging straight-line winds capable of reaching 60 mph. An isolated, weak tornado cannot be ruled out, particularly if any stronger rotating thunderstorms develop along the stationary boundary.

By tonight, showers and thunderstorms will gradually diminish, although additional scattered storms remain possible overnight into Saturday as the stalled boundary slowly begins lifting north. Rainfall coverage should become less widespread compared to today, but any storms that do develop will still be capable of producing locally heavy rainfall.

The weather pattern changes dramatically beginning Sunday. High pressure will strengthen across the central United States, shutting off rain chances and allowing a prolonged stretch of dangerous heat to settle over the region. Afternoon temperatures are expected to climb into the upper 90s through much of next week while dew points remain firmly in the 70s. This combination will push heat index values between 100 and 110 degrees, making this the hottest stretch of weather we've experienced so far this year. Heat Advisories will likely become necessary beginning Sunday, and portions of the area could eventually meet Excessive Heat Warning criteria if forecast trends continue.

Now is the time to prepare for both today's flooding threat and next week's heat. Never drive across flooded roadways, even if the water appears shallow, and remain alert for rapidly changing weather conditions throughout the day. Looking ahead to the heat, be sure to stay hydrated, limit strenuous outdoor activity during the afternoon, wear lightweight clothing, and check on elderly neighbors, children, and pets. The First Warn Weather Team will continue monitoring both today's flooding concerns and the developing heat wave through the weekend.
severeNarrative:
First Warn Forecast & Severe Weather Outlook Update
11:50 AM CDT | Wednesday, June 17, 2026
First Warn Weather Alert Day
Headlines
Severe thunderstorms remain likely this afternoon and evening across Missouri and Illinois.
Damaging winds up to 85 mph, large hail up to 2.5 inches, and several tornadoes remain possible.
The greatest threat locally remains across eastern and central Missouri and western Illinois, including the St. Louis metro area.
Strong southwest winds will gust between 40 and 50 mph outside of thunderstorms.
Peak severe weather threat for the St. Louis area remains between 6 PM and 9 PM.

A potentially dangerous severe weather outbreak remains on track across portions of Missouri and Illinois this afternoon and evening. There has been little change in the overall forecast since this morning. The Storm Prediction Center continues to highlight much of eastern and central Missouri and western Illinois in an Enhanced Risk (Level 3 of 5) for severe weather, while portions of central Illinois remain under a Moderate Risk (Level 4 of 5) where the highest threat for significant severe weather exists.

An intense cluster of thunderstorms that developed overnight across Iowa produced wind gusts exceeding 90 mph and continues to influence the forecast across Illinois late this morning. The primary outflow boundary currently extends from far northeast Missouri into portions of south-central Illinois. This boundary will likely serve as a focus for severe thunderstorm development later today.

Strong sunshine south of the boundary is allowing temperatures to rapidly climb through the 80s while dew points remain in the upper 60s and lower 70s. Combined with an exceptionally strong wind field aloft, conditions remain favorable for supercells capable of producing all severe weather hazards.

While morning storms may slightly alter where the greatest risk ultimately develops, confidence remains high that numerous severe thunderstorms will develop this afternoon and continue through the evening hours.

Primary Threats

🌪 Several Tornadoes Possible

A few strong tornadoes remain possible.
Greatest tornado threat remains across central Illinois.
Tornadoes remain possible across eastern Missouri and western Illinois, especially near any lingering outflow boundaries.

💨 Destructive Winds Possible

Widespread damaging winds of 60 to 80 mph.
Isolated wind gusts exceeding 85 mph remain possible.
Significant tree damage and widespread power outages could occur.

🧊 Large to Very Large Hail

Large hail remains possible with discrete supercells.
Hailstones up to 2.5 inches in diameter may occur.
Timing Across Missouri and Illinois
1 PM - 3 PM

The first severe thunderstorms are expected to develop across northeast Missouri and portions of west-central Illinois. Areas near Quincy, Kirksville, Palmyra, Hannibal, and surrounding communities should closely monitor conditions.

3 PM - 6 PM

The severe weather threat expands southeast into central Missouri and western Illinois. Columbia, Jefferson City, Mexico, Bowling Green, Troy, and portions of the northern St. Louis metro could see supercells capable of producing tornadoes, very large hail, and destructive winds.

6 PM - 9 PM

This remains the peak severe weather window for the St. Louis metropolitan area and much of eastern Missouri and western Illinois. Storms may become more widespread during this period while maintaining the potential to produce damaging winds, large hail, and tornadoes. Residents from St. Louis and St. Charles to Edwardsville, Belleville, Alton, Farmington, and surrounding communities should be prepared to act quickly if warnings are issued.

9 PM - Midnight

Storms are expected to organize into a line extending from central Missouri into southern Indiana. Damaging straight-line winds become the primary threat as storms shift into southeast Missouri and southern Illinois. Heavy rainfall and localized flooding may also develop overnight.

Local First Warn Outlook

The highest confidence for severe weather today exists across the Enhanced Risk area covering much of eastern and central Missouri, including Columbia, Jefferson City, the St. Louis metro, northeast Missouri, and west-central Illinois.

Farther south, a Slight Risk extends across southern Missouri and southern Illinois, including Rolla, Salem, Farmington, Fredericktown, Marion, and Mount Vernon. Severe thunderstorms remain possible in these areas, but storm coverage and intensity may be somewhat lower than locations farther north.

The lowest severe weather threat remains across the Missouri Bootheel and far southern Illinois near the Kentucky border where a Marginal Risk remains in place. However, isolated severe storms cannot be ruled out.

Threat Levels

🔴 Moderate Risk (Level 4 of 5)

Central Illinois
Areas northeast of Springfield, Illinois
Greatest potential for strong tornadoes and destructive winds

🟤 Enhanced Risk (Level 3 of 5)

Northeast Missouri
Central Missouri
Eastern Missouri
St. Louis Metro
West-central Illinois
Southwest Illinois

🟡 Slight Risk (Level 2 of 5)

Southern Missouri
South-central Missouri
Southeast Missouri
Southern Illinois
Rolla, Salem, Farmington, Fredericktown, Marion, and Mount Vernon

🟢 Marginal Risk (Level 1 of 5)

Missouri Bootheel
Far southern Illinois near the Kentucky border
Areas south of Poplar Bluff and near Paducah
Additional Concern: Strong Winds Outside of Storms

Even outside of thunderstorms, strong southwest winds will continue through the afternoon. Sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts of 40 to 50 mph are expected. Loose outdoor objects should be secured, and travel may become difficult for high-profile vehicles.

Thursday Outlook

The cold front responsible for today's severe weather will gradually move south of the region overnight. Showers and a few thunderstorms may linger into Thursday, especially across southeast Missouri and southwest Illinois. Cooler temperatures in the 70s and lower 80s will provide a welcome break from today's heat and humidity.

Bottom Line

There has been little change in the forecast thinking since this morning. The atmosphere remains primed for severe thunderstorm development this afternoon and evening. While the greatest threat for strong tornadoes remains across central Illinois, all severe weather hazards remain possible across eastern Missouri and western Illinois.

The St. Louis metro remains in the corridor for severe weather between 6 PM and 9 PM, with damaging winds, large hail, and tornadoes all possible. Everyone across the First Warn viewing area should remain weather aware and have multiple ways to receive warnings throughout the day and tonight.

First Warn Weather: First. Accurate. Trusted.
alertMessage: POTENTIAL SIGNIFICANT SEVERE WEATHER EVENT THIS AFTERNOON-TONIGHT.
